TURIMETTA BEACH

Sydney’s best-kept secret — the sundowner destination of a suburb that has not advertised this beach and does not intend to. The walk from Warriewood to Mona Vale Headland is 3km of cliff-top coastal scenery. The kind that renders the 26km from the CBD irrelevant.

WARRIEWOOD WETLANDS

2.4km of timber boardwalks through swamp mahogany forest — the largest remaining sand-plain wetland system in northern Sydney. A birdwatcher’s paradise. The retirees love the birds. The birds are indifferent to the retirees.

B-LINE

Express bus to Wynyard in 50 minutes — the suburb’s arterial connection to the city. 5G works the whole way. Used as a mobile office by the B-Line Professionals who have organised their lives around the surf and the swell window.

Warriewood

Northern Beaches

NSW 2102

Guringai Country

Character

A walk through Warriewood is a study in contrasts. Start at Warriewood Square: bustling retail hub. Move east toward the Valley: sleek, modern master-planned estates and townhouses that feel like a contemporary suburban utopia. Sharp, grey, glass-heavy architecture on perfectly paved cul-de-sacs where kids ride scooters. Move further east: steep cliffs of Turimetta Head, the Blowhole, Warriewood Beach.

The rhythm is the B-Line and the sun. At 6am, the Valley is a parade of commuters walking toward Pittwater Road with coffee. Weekends: the wetland boardwalks and the cliff tracks. The suburb that feels like a quiet country town that accidentally collided with a modern coastal development — and decided both were worth keeping.

 

The great paradox: a beach suburb where a significant population lives in a lush, humid valley overlooking a swamp, three minutes from some of the most aggressive surf on the coast. The Blowhole at the north end. The wetland boardwalk five minutes west. Turimetta Beach — Sydney’s best-kept secret — for the sundowner walk. The contrasts are the suburb’s identity, not its unresolved problem.

Warriewood is losing its industrial backyard feel and becoming a polished, premium residential enclave. It is no longer the affordable neighbour to Mona Vale. The retirees miss the old market gardens. The apartments continue to arrive. The wetlands remain protected.

26
Distance from CBD
26km
B-Line to Wynyard in 50 minutes — used as a mobile office. 5G works the whole way. 2.1 cars per household for everything else. Median tenure 8.5 years — newer suburb, faster turnover, steadily rising prices.
19
Ocean temperature
19°C
Warriewood Beach and Turimetta Beach — consistent Northern Beaches ocean swimming. The Blowhole: heavy right-handers for experienced surfers. The beach that the suburb has not advertised and does not intend to.
Top
Growth percentile
Top 15%
Median house $2.45M (+32% since 2021). Unit $1.35M (+24%). Yield 3.1% — strongest on the Northern Beaches. The suburb that was affordable, then wasn’t, and is now simply competitive.
39
Median age
39yrs
Population ~9,500. 22% renting. 18% living alone. 34% tertiary educated. Median HH income $2,850/wk. Youngest median age of the Northern Beaches suburbs in this study — the B-Line Professionals have arrived.
A seamless blend of wild wetlands and golden sands where the rugged Valley meets modern coastal living — and Turimetta Beach remains the Northern Beaches’ best-kept secret.

Safety
Who lives here

The B-Line Professional

Young Couples, CBD Workers

Refuse to sacrifice the surf. Live in the new apartments and value the 50-minute express bus. The B-Line is their office. Arrive at Wynyard before finishing the podcast. Surf before the first meeting.

The Multi-Gen Migrant

Descendants of Glasshouse Farmers

Often own larger, older plots on the valley fringe. The longest-tenured landowners in the suburb. Remember the greenhouses. Have watched the master-planned estates arrive around their land with the particular equanimity of people whose family has been here longest.

The Active Empty-Nester

Downsizers from North Shore

Moved from St Ives or Pymble to luxury townhouses for a lock-up-and-leave lifestyle near the ocean. Found in Warriewood’s Valley the contemporary suburban utopia that the North Shore never quite was.

The Surf-Stoked Family

Local Families

Prioritise being in the Narrabeen Sports High catchment and having enough garage space for four surfboards and two e-bikes. Found in Warriewood the suburb that accommodates this specific configuration without requiring a compromise.

Life in Warriewood

2026
01

Warriewood Square

The functional heart — bustling retail hub where the suburb does its errands. Best retail on the Northern Beaches alongside Mona Vale. The Square is where the B-Line Professionals stop before the bus and where the Surf-Stoked Families do the weekly shop.

02

Food & Drink

Every day — Chill-out Cafe and Sunrise Cafe at the surf club: the everyday soul of the suburb. The occasion: The Newport or Pasadena at Church Point for the long lunch. The community: Beaches Market (Friday, Pittwater Park) — the weekly ritual that makes the suburb feel like a town.

03

Warriewood Wetlands

2.4km of timber boardwalks through swamp mahogany forest — the largest remaining sand-plain wetland system in northern Sydney. A birdwatcher’s paradise and a serene escape from the summer heat. The birds are indifferent to both demographics who come to see them.

04

Turimetta Beach

Sydney’s best-kept secret — the sundowner destination of a suburb that has not advertised this beach and does not intend to. The walk from Warriewood to Mona Vale Headland: 3km, cliff-top, whale watching spots. The kind of coastal scenery that renders the 26km from the CBD irrelevant.

05

The Blowhole

At the north end of the beach — consistent and often heavy right-handers preferred by the suburb’s more experienced surfers. The daily office for those who have organised their professional lives around the swell window. Warriewood SLSC: the heart of the community, the nippers every weekend.

06

2026 Update

The Macpherson Street Bridge upgrade complete — historic flooding issues eliminated. Warriewood Community Centre redesigned with co-working spaces for the WFH crowd. Southern industrial edge rezoning announced: breweries and boutique fitness mirroring the Brookvale model.

Resident Voices

01 — Commuter
B-Line Professional
Loves it
The B-Line is my office. I get a seat, 5G works the whole way, and I’m in Wynyard before I finish my podcast. This sentence is not an exaggeration. I have verified it every working day for two years.
Frustrates
The traffic on Mona Vale Road on Saturdays is a parking lot. I have learned to time my errands around this. My knowledge of Saturday traffic patterns is detailed and entirely unwanted.
02 — Teenager
Local
Loves it
It’s sick being so close to the beach and the Square. We just e-bike everywhere. The beach in the morning, the Square in the afternoon, the beach again. This is the entire schedule. I am satisfied with it.
Frustrates
Wish there was more to do at night other than hanging out at the Maccas car park. This is an honest assessment of the situation and I offer it without embarrassment.
03 — Retiree
Long-Term
Loves it
I love the birds in the wetlands. It’s so quiet compared to the city. The kookaburras at 6am. The ibis in the swamp mahogany. The boardwalk at dawn. I have not once questioned the decision to move here.
Frustrates
All these new apartments are making the area feel crowded. I miss the old market gardens. I know why they are gone. I miss them anyway. Both things are true and I hold them simultaneously.

The Glasshouse District — now a destination in its own right

2026 Update

No Longer the Affordable Neighbour

Warriewood is completing its transition from industrial backyard to polished premium residential enclave. The Glasshouse District is now a destination. The market has re-rated it. The infrastructure is following. The wetlands remain protected. Turimetta Beach remains secret.

Macpherson St Bridge — Complete
Road upgrade complete — historic flooding issues eliminated. The infrastructure failure that used to cut the suburb in half during heavy rain has been resolved. The suburb is now continuous in all weather.
Community Centre — Redesigned
State-of-the-art co-working spaces for the WFH crowd — the community infrastructure following the demographic that has arrived in the Valley over the past four years.
Industrial Rezoning — Announced
Southern industrial edge rezoned for creative-industrial use: breweries and boutique fitness, mirroring the Brookvale model. The industrial backyard being converted into the lifestyle hub its new residents are expecting.
Liveability: Domain 2026Prices: CoreLogic / Domain Apr 2026Census: ABS 2021Crime: BOCSAR 2025Schools: MySchool 2025
